title-group.article-title: Successful adalimumab graded challenge after allergic delayed reaction to golimumab in a woman with rheumatoid arthritis

aff.institution: Fondazione IRCCS Ca’ Granda Ospedale Maggiore Policlinico

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence Federica Rivolta, Department of General Medicine Immunology and Allergology, IRCCS Foundation Ca’ Granda Ospedale Maggiore Policlinico, Milan, Italy and General Medicine, Allergology and Clinical Immunology, University of Milan, Via Pace, 9, 20122 Milano, MI, Italy. Email: federica.rivolta@policlinico.mi.it

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Authors. Clinical Case Reports published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: A graded challenge with adalimumab could be safe in case of a delayed allergic reaction to golimumab, after a detailed allergological evaluation and the exclusion of allergic sensitization using skin tests.

A graded challenge with adalimumab could be safe in case of a delayed allergic reaction to golimumab, after a detailed allergological evaluation and the exclusion of allergic sensitization using skin tests.

p.mixed-citation: RivoltaF, SangalliA, CappellettiC, Chiei GalloA, DubiniM, PravettoniV. Successful adalimumab graded challenge after allergic delayed reaction to golimumab in a woman with rheumatoid arthritis. Clin Case Rep. 2023;11:e7067. doi:10.1002/ccr3.7067PMC1003730236968346

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: We describe the case of a patient with a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) experiencing a mucocutaneous delayed reaction to golimumab. Anyway, there is no consensus regarding the best management of delayed reactions to biologics. Excluding allergic cross‐reactivity or sensitization, we finally tested adalimumab by a graded challenge.

Tumor necrosis factor (TNF)‐α is a pro‐inflammatory cytokine with homeostatic and biological pleiotropic effects. 1 However, it is also a cytokine involved in pathological conditions, such as organ injury in some immune‐mediated inflammatory diseases. For this reason, in the past few years, research has been focused on the blockade of its dangerous effects. As a result, a number of biologic agents directed against TNFα has been developed, preventing the interaction with its receptors TNFR1 and TNFR2. Adalimumab (Humira®) and golimumab (Simponi®) are two of these monoclonal antibodies used in the treatment of RA and other inflammatory diseases: both of them are fully human IgG1 monoclonal anti‐TNF antibodies. The structure of each of these biologic agents consists of two unique and identical heavy chains and two unique and identical light chains. The Fc regions are identical, whereas the N‐terminal domains are different and assure the specificity of each monoclonal antibody. 2 , 3

The study of the molecular binding site to the TNFα has demonstrated that adalimumab directly overlaps the TNFR2‐binding area in an extensive manner, causing a steric blocking that interferes the ligand–receptor interaction, 4 whereas golimumab binds to a distinct epitope on TNFα that does not overlap with the binding residues of TNFR2. Thus, golimumab efficacy is granted by the steric hindrance, though partial, that interferes between the TNF α and TNFR1‐2. Compared to the other anti‐TNFα agents, this mechanism of action is peculiar to golimumab. 5
Despite the frequent use of anti‐TNF agents, their side effects are rare and typically include local injection site reactions, infections, immune‐mediated disorders, and malignancies. 6

Hypersensitivity reactions (HSRs) can occur with all available biologic agents. The risk of serious HSRs is higher during the first or second infusion and during intravenous administration compared to subcutaneous one. HSRs can be divided into acute (immediate) and delayed. Delayed reactions usually occur from 1 to 14 days after drug administration and commonly resemble serum sickness. 7

Although the prevalence of acute and delayed reactions to anti‐TNF agents is relatively low, both types of reactions constitute a serious clinical problem because of its severity and the subsequent need for discontinuation of the treatment. Little is known about the precise mechanisms underlying these reactions, including a possible role of anti‐drug antibodies. 8
In this article, we report a case of a delayed HSR to golimumab, possibly caused by the development of a T‐cell‐mediated response.

sec.title: CASE REPORT
sec.p: We describe the case of a 46‐year‐old woman with a history of autoimmune uveitis and a diagnosis of RA since 2019, based on symmetrical polidistrectual arthritis and positivity to rheumatoid factor. During puberty and early adulthood, she suffered from allergic asthma and oculorhinitis. RA was initially treated with methotrexate for 30 months with remission of articular involvement. Due to several relapses of uveitis and the need of corticosteroids, the rheumatologist prescribed subcutaneous golimumab as biological treatment. After the second infusion, 12 h later, the patient experienced a delayed mucocutaneous reaction, consisting of labial, perioral, and tongue angioedema, spontaneously resolved the following day. Based on the ocular predominant involvement, adalimumab was considered as the best alternative choice. Therefore, given the possible cross‐reactivity between golimumab and adalimumab due to their identical structure, the patient was referred for an allergological evaluation to our Allergy Unit.

After accurate clinical history taking and signing of the informed consent by the patient, skin tests were performed using adalimumab (Humira®) at the concentration of 40 mg/mL for skin prick test (SPT) and dilutions of 1:1000, 1:100, and 1:10 for subsequent intradermal test (IDT) with 0.02 mL of each solution. SPT and IDT were all negative. Histamine and saline were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. The patient was discharged asymptomatic after 3 h of observation following the last IDT. The patient was also checked for delayed reaction during the next 72 h. 9

A week after the cutaneous testing, we performed challenge test with adalimumab (Humira® 40 mg). First, we administered the drug in a single day in five steps at incremental doses every 30 min subcutaneously, until the final dose of 40 mg was reached (Table 1). The patient was taken under observation for 3 h after the last injection and then discharged asymptomatic and no reaction occurred.

A week later, we performed a second controlled administration of adalimumab (Humira® 40 mg) in a single dose of 40 mg. The administration was well tolerated, and the patient was discharged after 4 h without any reaction.

Consequently, the patient began therapy with subcutaneous adalimumab, without developing any adverse or allergic reaction. In the following 12 months, the patient well tolerated the tested drug and had no relapse of uveitis or other RA comorbidities.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Biologic agents are crucial targeted drugs also used in immune‐mediated inflammatory diseases. Their spread has been increasing on daily practice, especially in personalized precision medicine.
Consequently, even if they are generally well tolerated, the increased utilization may lead to increase occurrence of HSRs.
Furthermore, the optimal management of delayed reactions is still a matter of discussion. 9

The reaction to golimumab affecting our patient was considered as delayed, after excluding an immediate reaction or an anti‐drug antibody mediated one, according to the EAACI position paper on hypersensitivity to biologicals. 9

Due to the availability of another biologic agent, considering the 2020 review on HSRs to biologic agents, 10 it was decided to replace the biological drug in question with another anti‐TNF agent, that is, adalimumab. To exclude cross‐reactivity between the two biologics, we performed cutaneous tests, that resulted completely negative, both for immediate and for delayed reactions.
Because of the small number of cases in the literature, there is no standard graded challenge procedure for allergy to biologics.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: The aim of this report is to strengthen the idea that a graded challenge with adalimumab could be a safe option in case of a delayed allergic reaction to golimumab, after a detailed allergological evaluation and the exclusion of allergic sensitization using skin tests. This protocol could be applied for other kind of biologic agents.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Federica Rivolta: Investigation; methodology. Andrea Sangalli: Conceptualization; data curation; formal analysis; investigation; writing – original draft. Camilla Cappelletti: Investigation; writing – original draft. Alessandra Chiei Gallo: Writing – original draft. Marco Dubini: Methodology; writing – original draft. Valerio Pravettoni: Investigation; methodology; supervision; validation.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: This study was partially funded by Italian Ministry of Health, Current research IRCCS.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors certify that there is no conflict of interest with any financial organization regarding the material discussed in the manuscript.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: All procedures were in accordance with the ethical standards of the responsible committee on human experimentation (institutional and national) and with the Helsinki Declaration of 1975, as revised in 2013. Informed written consent was obtained from the patient.

sec.title: CONSENT
sec.p: Written informed consent was obtained from the patient to publish this report in accordance with the journal's patient consent policy.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
